title: Chapter 12: Greg Quits
content: The crying stopped about four minutes ago. Chloe knows because she counts things like that now — not because she wants to, but because her brain does it automatically, the way it used to count deliverables and action items and follow-up deadlines. Old habits. Dumb old habits.

Now there's just quiet. The specific kind of quiet that lives in a room after someone has emptied themselves out in it. The walls hold it — a soft, damp hush, like the moment after a thunderstorm passes and the world remembers how to be still.

Greg is sitting on the floor, cross-legged, like a kindergartner at story time. Chloe's head is in his lap. Has been for — she doesn't know. A while. His hand moves over her fur in slow, automatic strokes. He's not really petting her anymore. He's just moving. Keeping his hands busy so his brain doesn't find something worse to do.
Chloe hums.

It's not a conscious thing. It never is. The hum rises from somewhere deep in her chest, a low, resonant note that fills the space between the floorboards and the ceiling, between Greg's ragged breathing and Maya's careful silence on the couch. The hum doesn't fix anything. It's not supposed to. It just makes the room slightly more tolerable, the way a candle makes a dark room slightly less dark. You don't curse the candle for not being the sun.
Greg's hand pauses on her back. Rests there. Flat palm, warm through her fur.
"I haven't slept more than five hours since March," he says.
His voice is flat. Not dramatic. Not seeking sympathy. Just a data point, delivered to the room like a weather report. Currently 150 over 95 with a chance of complete cardiovascular collapse.

"Blood pressure's 150 over 95. Pre-hypertensive. Doctor told me to cut back on coffee." A sound leaves him that might be trying to be a laugh. It doesn't make it. "Cut back on coffee. Like that's the problem. Like the problem is lattes and not the fact that I've been running on cortisol and spite for eight months."
Nobody says anything.

Chloe hums a little lower. Not because she's trying to shift the vibe. Because her body responds to distress the way a furnace responds to cold — automatically, without consultation. Someone in the room is hurting. The capybara engine engages. Maximum comfort output.
Greg's thumb traces a small circle behind her ear. His breathing evens out just slightly. Not enough. But slightly.
"I had a panic attack in the Staples parking lot last Tuesday," he says. "A *Staples* parking lot. I was there to buy printer paper. That's what my life is. Panic attacks about office supplies."

Maya shifts on the couch. Chloe can hear it — the creak of the cushion, the soft weight redistribution. Maya, who is normally a font of pithy observations and devastating one-liners, has been quiet for almost fifteen minutes. That's how they all know it's serious. The room has taken Maya's voice as collateral.

"I pulled over," Greg continues, "and I sat in my car for forty minutes, and I thought — this is it. This is the whole thing. This is the entire deal. I'm going to spend the next thirty years buying printer paper and having panic attacks in parking lots and texting people ' circling back!' like it means something."
He takes a breath. Chloe feels it move through him, the slight expansion of his ribcage, the shaky exhale.
"And then I came here. And I sat on the floor. And your capybara put her head in my lap." A beat. "And this is the calmest I've felt in seven months."
Chloe blinks. She is a capybara. She has done nothing except exist in proximity. This is her whole skill set. Existence. Proximity. Humming.
It is, apparently, enough.

The silence stretches. Not the bad kind. The kind that has texture, weight. The kind you could fold up and put in your pocket. Chloe's hum fills it, warm and woolen, a sound-blanket for a room that desperately needed one.
Then Greg moves.

Not stands — not yet. Just shifts. Reaches into his back pocket with his free hand. Pulls out his phone. The screen glows in the dim apartment, casting his face in pale blue light. His expression doesn't change much. It doesn't need to. His fingers move across the screen with a deliberateness that feels different from the frantic, notification-dodging swiping he was doing when he arrived.
He opens Slack.
Types.

Chloe can't read what he's writing — she's a capybara, not a literary critic — but she can feel the way his body changes as he types. The tension in his shoulders. The slight tremor in the thumb that's not scrolling. The way his breathing goes shallow and then, right before he finishes, deep. Deliberately deep. Like he's jumping off something.
He turns the phone around.
The room reads it:

> I'm resigning, effective immediately. Good luck with the Meridian account.
> — Greg
His thumb hovers over the send button for exactly one second. Then he presses it.

The room does not explode. There is no dramatic music. No cinematic zoom. Just a soft whoosh sound — Slack sending a message, a small digital exhalation — and then Greg lowers the phone to his lap and stares at the ceiling.
Maya finds her voice. Naturally, it returns in the form of a question.
"Did you just quit your job on the floor of our apartment while petting a capybara?"
Greg doesn't look at her. "Yeah."
"That's the most Brooklyn thing that's ever happened in this apartment."

"And we once saw a guy walk a ferret on the subway," Dave adds from the kitchen doorway. He's holding a mug of something herbal. He's been silently hovering for the last ten minutes, which is very Dave — present but not intrusive, like a really supportive lamp.

Greg laughs. A real one. Short, startled, like it surprised him on the way out. "I just — I couldn't." He shakes his head. "I couldn't do one more day of pretending that any of it mattered. The Meridian account. The Q3 deliverables. The *alignment meetings*. None of it —" He stops. Swallows. "None of it was worth the Staples parking lot."
Chloe hums.
It's a slightly different note now. Deeper. Rounder. The kind of hum that says: *Yes. You did a hard thing. I am here. The floor is also here. It's fine.*

Greg's phone starts to vibrate. Then it vibrates again. And again. Notifications cascading like a slot machine that only pays out in anxiety — Slack reactions, emails, the increasingly frantic digital flailing of a workplace that just lost its workhorse. He watches it for a moment, the screen lighting up with each new arrival, a small rectangular panic attack.
Then he holds down the power button.
The phone goes dark.
He sets it on the floor, screen-down, like he's putting a child to sleep. Or maybe a demon. Hard to tell with corporate communication.
Greg looks down at Chloe. She looks up at him — as much as she can with her head in his lap, which isn't much, so mostly she just looks at his chin.
"Thank you," he says.

He doesn't specify who he's thanking. Chloe? The room? The vibes? Ancient capybara energy that has flowed through his ancestors since the Pleistocene? Doesn't matter. The gratitude is in the air now, diffuse and directionless, and the hum absorbs it like soil absorbs rain.

Greg slides out from under her — gently, carefully, supporting her head so it lands on the rug instead of the floor. He stands. He's taller than Chloe realized. Or maybe he just takes up more space when he's not actively compressing himself into a corporate shape.
At the door, he turns back.

"Can I come back?" he asks. "Not for — not for work. Just to..." He gestures at Chloe. The gesture is vague and encompassing and means approximately: *sit on your floor and experience tranquility via large rodent.*
Maya smiles. Actually smiles, not her usual sardonic half-smirk. "She's here every day. She doesn't have a schedule."

Greg almost smiles. It's a close thing. The almost-smile hovers at the edges of his mouth like a bird that's thinking about landing but isn't sure the surface is stable. It doesn't quite touch down. But it considers it.
"Okay," he says. "Okay."
He leaves.

The door clicks shut, and the apartment folds back into its default state of slightly-chaotic quiet. Dave returns to the kitchen. Maya picks up her sketchpad. The normality reassembles itself around Greg's absence like water filling a footprint.
Chloe lies on the rug.

She should feel satisfied. And she does — genuinely, fully, in her capybara bones. A person came into this room in pieces and left in one piece. The hum worked. The vibes worked. The ancient capybara energy of the Pleistocene continues to deliver results.
But.

There is a small sharp note underneath the satisfaction. A splinter, buried just below the surface of the good feeling. She hums and the splinter hums back, a dissonant frequency, barely audible but definitely there.
Greg was made by the machine.

Not created — *made.* Forged and pressed and assembled by the same system of deliverables and alignment meetings and Q3 objectives that nearly broke Chloe. The same machine that runs on human stress responses and outputs quarterly growth. Greg was raw material and the machine processed him and then the machine spit him out into a Staples parking lot to have a panic attack about printer paper.
Greg quit.
Greg quit and turned off his phone and almost smiled and walked out the door. Greg is free, or at least freer, or at least heading in the direction of free.
But the machine didn't quit.

The machine is still there. Right now, somewhere in a building with too much glass and not enough houseplants, someone is already looking at Greg's Slack message and thinking about coverage plans. Someone is reassigning the Meridian account. Someone is updating an org chart. The machine does not grieve its parts. It replaces them.
Chloe's hum dips — just for a moment — into a register that isn't quite comfort. It's something else. Something with teeth.
Then it passes. The hum returns to its baseline. The splinter stays.
Satisfaction: 🔔🔔🔔
Large. Real. Greg quitting, Greg turning off his phone, Greg almost smiling on his way out the door. A victory. A genuine, fur-warmed, floor-seated victory.
But the machine persists.
And Chloe, for the first time since she started humming, isn't sure the hum is enough.
